acceptedAnswer.text: MACRO Photography captures very small subjects at high magnification. True macro is 1:1 reproduction—life-size on the camera sensor—revealing detail the eye often misses.

mainEntity.name: What magnification counts as true macro?

acceptedAnswer.text: 1:1 magnification (life-size on the sensor) is true macro. Close-up lenses may offer less (e.g., 1:2), which is still useful but not true macro.

mainEntity.name: How do I get sharp focus in MACRO Photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use a tripod, manual focus with Live View, and a remote or timer. For moving subjects, try AF-C or back-button focus and raise shutter speed.

mainEntity.name: What lighting works best for macro?

acceptedAnswer.text: Diffuse light to avoid harsh speculars. Use LED or flash with a diffuser. Position light from the side to reveal texture; keep backgrounds distant for separation.

mainEntity.name: What aperture and shutter speed should I start with?

acceptedAnswer.text: Begin around ISO 100, f/8–f/16 for depth, and adjust shutter speed to maintain sharpness. Handheld often needs 1/250s+; tripod allows slower speeds.
Shooting a series at different focus points and blending them to extend depth of field—ideal for static subjects when single-frame DOF is too shallow.

mainEntity.name: What is working distance and why does it matter?

acceptedAnswer.text: Working distance is the space from lens front to subject. More distance reduces shadows, eases lighting, and avoids disturbing insects or delicate subjects.
